questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums
questions.structural_anchor: Derivation of sum of arithmetic sequence
questions.question: Find the sum of the first 20 natural numbers: \(1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + 20\).
questions.explanation: \(S_{20} = \frac{20 \times 21}{2} = 10 \times 21 = 210\). Option A is the sum of the first 19 numbers (\(19 \times 20 / 2 = 190\)), option B is \(20 \times 20 / 2 = 200\).

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums
source_trace.structural_element: Derivation of sum of arithmetic sequence
source_trace.content_anchor: sum of first n natural numbers = n(n+1)/2

explanations_tiered.definition: The sum of the first n natural numbers is given by \(S_n = n(n+1)/2\).

formula_analysis.name: Sum of first n natural numbers
formula_analysis.derivation: Gauss method: write sum forward and backward, add, get n copies of (n+1), then divide by 2.

misconceptions: Using n(n+1) without dividing by 2: 20×21=420 (not an option).
Using (n-1)n/2 = 19×20/2=190 (option A, sum of first 19).

glossary_entry.definition: The sum of the first n positive integers is \(n(n+1)/2\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Mathematics Unit 1 — Sum of Arithmetic Series
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The sum of the first n positive integers is n(n+1)/2.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Introduce Gauss's childhood story to engage students.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compute the partial sum of some sequences
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. \(S_{20}=20×21/2=210\). Option A is sum of first 19.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums
questions.question: An arithmetic sequence has first term \(A_1 = 5\) and common difference \(d = 3\). Find the sum of the first 15 terms, \(S_{15}\).
questions.explanation: \(S_{15} = \frac{15}{2}[2\times5 + (15-1)\times3] = \frac{15}{2}[10 + 42] = \frac{15}{2} \times 52 = 15 \times 26 = 390\). Option C (360) uses \(n=15\) but miscalculates \(2A_1 + (n-1)d = 10+42=52\), then \(15×52/2=390\) correct. 360 would be \(15×48/2\) or \(15×24=360\) with different numbers.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums

explanations_tiered.definition: The sum of the first n terms of an arithmetic sequence is \(S_n = \frac{n}{2}(2A_1 + (n-1)d)\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: List first few: 5,8,11,14,... but faster: use formula. 2×5=10, plus 14×3=42, sum=52, times 15 = 780, divided by 2 = 390.
misconceptions: Using \(S_n = \frac{n}{2}(A_1 + A_n)\) but forgetting to compute \(A_{15}\) correctly: \(A_{15}=5+14×3=47\), \(15(5+47)/2=15×52/2=390\) correct. No error. Option 360 would be \(15(5+43)/2\) with \(A_{15}=43\) (using 13×3 instead of 14).
Forgetting to multiply by n before dividing by 2.

glossary_entry.definition: The sum of the first n terms of an arithmetic sequence.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The sum of the terms of an arithmetic sequence.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce both forms of the sum formula.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compute the partial sum of some sequences
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums
questions.question: Find the sum of the first 6 terms of the geometric sequence \(3, 6, 12, 24, \dots\).
questions.explanation: \(S_6 = \frac{3(2^6 - 1)}{2-1} = 3(64-1) = 3 \times 63 = 189\). Option C (126) is the sum of the first 5 terms (\(3+6+12+24+48=93\)?) Wait, 3+6=9, +12=21, +24=45, +48=93, +96=189. 126 would be 3+6+12+24+48+... no. 126 is 2×63, but formula misuse: \(3(2^6)/2 = 3×64/2=96\) not 126. Option A (63) is \(3(2^5-1)=3×31=93\)? No, 63 is 2^6-1? 64-1=63, times 3 =189 not 63. Actually 63 is the sum of first 5 of 1,2,4,8,16? Or 3×21? Confusion. Let me recalc: Sum = 3+6+12+24+48+96 = 189. Option A 63 is sum of 3+6+12+24+18? No. Option B 93 is sum of first 5. Option C 126 is half of 252? Possibly r=2 but n=5 gives 93, n=6 gives 189. So D is correct.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums

explanations_tiered.definition: The sum of the first n terms of a geometric sequence is given by \(S_n = G_1(r^n - 1)/(r-1)\) for \(r \neq 1\).

formula_analysis.name: Sum of geometric sequence
formula_analysis.derivation: Multiply S_n by r, subtract, solve for S_n.

misconceptions: Using \(r^n\) instead of \(r^n-1\): \(3×64=192\) (close to 189 but not exact).
Using denominator \(1-r\) for r>1 gives negative sum if not careful.

glossary_entry.definition: The sum of the first n terms of a geometric sequence.

explanations_tiered.workbook_section: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums
questions.structural_anchor: Further on the sum of geometric sequences
questions.question: The sum of the first three terms of a geometric sequence is 7, and the sum of the next three terms is 56. If the common ratio \(r > 0\), what is the value of \(r\)?
questions.hint: Let the first term be a. Then \(a + ar + ar^2 = 7\) and \(ar^3 + ar^4 + ar^5 = 56 = r^3(a + ar + ar^2)\).
questions.explanation: From \(r^3 \times 7 = 56 \Rightarrow r^3 = 8 \Rightarrow r = 2\) (since \(r>0\)). Option B (3) would give \(r^3=27\), 27×7=189 not 56.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums
source_trace.structural_element: Further on the sum of geometric sequences
source_trace.content_anchor: sum first three terms = 7, sum next three = 56, find r

explanations_tiered.definition: In a geometric sequence, terms are \(a, ar, ar^2, ar^3, ar^4, ar^5, \dots\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The sum of terms 4-6 is \(r^3\) times the sum of terms 1-3. Thus \(7r^3 = 56 \Rightarrow r^3=8 \Rightarrow r=2\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: The next three terms are each multiplied by \(r^3\) compared to the first three, so \(7 × r^3 = 56\), so \(r^3=8\), \(r=2\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Factor out \(r^3\): \(ar^3 + ar^4 + ar^5 = r^3(a + ar + ar^2) = r^3 \times 7 = 56 \Rightarrow r^3=8 \Rightarrow r=2\).

formula_analysis.name: Sum of first three geometric terms

misconceptions: Adding the sums instead of factoring: \(7+56=63\) then trying to relate to \(r\).
Solving \(r^3=56\) directly without dividing by 7.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Next k terms = \(r^k\) × first k terms.

glossary_entry.definition: In a geometric sequence, the ratio between the sum of any block of consecutive terms and the previous block of same length is \(r^k\).

flashcard.front: If sum of first 3 terms = 7 and next 3 = 56 in a geometric sequence, find r.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Sum first 3=7, next 3=56 in geometric. Find r.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Sum of terms k+1 to 2k equals r^k times sum of terms 1 to k.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Block sums: \(S_{next block} = r^k \cdot S_{first block}\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: This is an elegant property that avoids solving for a explicitly.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compute the partial sum of some sequences
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 1.3 The Sigma Notation and Partial Sums
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. \(r^3 = 56/7 = 8 \Rightarrow r=2\). Option B (3) gives 7×27=189.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: The infinite geometric series \(\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^k\) converges. What is its sum?
questions.hint: The series is \(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{8} + \cdots\). First term \(a = \frac{1}{2}\), ratio \(r = \frac{1}{2}\). Use \(S_\infty = \frac{a}{1-r}\).
questions.explanation: \(S_\infty = \frac{1/2}{1-1/2} = \frac{1/2}{1/2} = 1\). Option A is the first term only, option D would be if \(a=1\) and \(r=1/2\) gives 2, which is not the case.

explanations_tiered.definition: An infinite geometric series converges to \(a/(1-r)\) when \(|r|<1\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 + ... = 1. Think of a whole pizza: half plus quarter plus eighth etc. adds to one whole.
misconceptions: Taking \(a=1\) instead of \(1/2\), giving sum = 2 (option D).
Using \(S = a/(1-r)\) with \(r>1\) (not the case).

glossary_entry.definition: The sum of an infinite geometric progression, converging to \(a/(1-r)\) if \(|r|<1\).

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A series of the form a + ar + ar² + ... converging to a/(1-r) when |r|<1.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Visualize with a square or circle being halved repeatedly.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compute the infinite sum of some sequences

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which of the following infinite geometric series diverges because its common ratio satisfies \(|r| \ge 1\)?
questions.hint: Check the absolute value of the common ratio for each series.
questions.explanation: For \(\sum r^k\), convergence requires \(|r| < 1\). Option C has \(r = 5/4 = 1.25 > 1\), so it diverges. Options A, B, D have \(|r| < 1\) and converge.

explanations_tiered.definition: A geometric series converges if the absolute value of the common ratio is less than 1.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Only option C has \(|r| = 5/4 > 1\), so it diverges.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you multiply by a number greater than 1 each time, the terms get bigger and bigger, so the sum never stops growing → diverges.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Convergence test: \(|r|<1\). A: 3/4<1 converge; B: 2/3<1 converge; C: 5/4>1 diverge; D: 1/10<1 converge.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The nth partial sum \(S_n = a(1-r^n)/(1-r)\). If \(|r|>1\), \(|r^n|\to\infty\), so \(S_n\) does not approach a finite limit.
reasoning_steps: For each option, identify common ratio r.
If |r| < 1, series converges; if |r| ≥ 1, series diverges.
Only C has |5/4| > 1, so it diverges.
misconceptions: Thinking that negative r always converges (false: if |r|≥1, it diverges or vibrates).
Assuming that r=5/4 is less than 1 because it's a fraction (it's >1).

glossary_entry.definition: A series whose partial sums do not approach a finite limit.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A series that does not converge to a finite sum.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that divergence includes oscillation (r≤-1) and unbounded growth (r>1).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify convergence and divergence of infinite series
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. r=5/4 >1 → diverges. Others have |r|<1 and converge.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Converting purely recurring decimals to fraction
questions.question: Convert the recurring decimal \(0.\overline{123}\) to a fraction.
questions.hint: If the repeating block has 3 digits, the denominator is \(10^3 - 1 = 999\).
questions.explanation: Let \(x = 0.123123123\ldots\). Then \(1000x = 123.123123\ldots = 123 + x \Rightarrow 999x = 123 \Rightarrow x = \frac{123}{999}\). Option B is the terminating decimal, C has denominator 99 (for 2-digit repeat), D is for one digit repeat? No, 123/900 = 0.13666... not correct.
formula_used: If repeating block has n digits, denominator = \(10^n - 1\)

calculation_steps: Simplify: divide numerator and denominator by 3 → 41/333

source_trace.structural_element: Converting purely recurring decimals to fraction

explanations_tiered.definition: A purely recurring decimal with an n-digit repeating block converts to a fraction with denominator \(10^n - 1\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For 3 repeating digits, denominator is 999, numerator is the repeating block 123.
misconceptions: Using denominator 1000 for terminating decimal (option B).
Using denominator 99 (option C) for a 3-digit repeat.

glossary_entry.definition: A decimal where the repeating block starts immediately after the decimal point.

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A decimal with a repeating pattern of digits.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Generalize: for n repeating digits, denominator = 10^n - 1.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Insert three arithmetic means between 4 and 20. What is the second arithmetic mean?
questions.hint: The sequence will be: 4, A2, A3, A4, 20. There are 5 terms total. Find common difference d.
questions.explanation: With 5 terms, \(A_5 = A_1 + 4d \Rightarrow 20 = 4 + 4d \Rightarrow 4d = 16 \Rightarrow d=4\). The sequence: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20. The second arithmetic mean (A3? Actually A2 is first mean, A3 is second mean) is 12. Option A is first mean, C is third mean, D is incorrect.

source_trace.content_anchor: Insert four arithmetic means between 4 and 14

explanations_tiered.definition: Arithmetic means between two numbers are terms that, together with the endpoints, form an arithmetic sequence.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: With 3 means, total 5 terms. d = (20-4)/(5-1)=16/4=4. Sequence: 4,8,12,16,20. The second mean is the 3rd term = 12.
explanations_tiered.beginner: List the sequence: start 4, end 20, need 3 numbers in between equally spaced. The difference is (20-4)/4 = 4, so 4,8,12,16,20. The second inserted number is 12.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Let d be common difference. Then 4 + 4d = 20 → 4d=16 → d=4. Terms: 4,8,12,16,20. The second mean = 12.
explanations_tiered.advanced: General formula: A_k = A_1 + (k-1)d with d = (b-a)/(n+1) where n = number of means. Here n=3, d=(20-4)/4=4. Second mean = 4 + (2)×4 = 12.
misconceptions: Counting total terms incorrectly: 3 means + 2 ends = 5 terms, so d = (20-4)/(5-1)=4 correct. Some may use n=3 means, d=16/3 not integer.
Taking the first mean (8) instead of second (12).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: n means between a and b: d = (b-a)/(n+1).

glossary_entry.definition: Terms inserted between two numbers to form an arithmetic sequence.

flashcard.front: Insert 3 arithmetic means between 4 and 20. What is the second mean?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Three arithmetic means between 4 and 20: second mean?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Terms that, with given endpoints, form an arithmetic sequence.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that the number of intervals is one more than the number of means.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use given terms to develop a formula that represents the sequence
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Total 5 terms. d=16/4=4. Sequence: 4,8,12,16,20. Second mean = 12.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Find the positive geometric mean between 5 and 45.
questions.hint: For two positive numbers, the geometric mean is \(\sqrt{ab}\).
questions.explanation: \(\sqrt{5 \times 45} = \sqrt{225} = 15\). Option A is \(\sqrt{81}\) (if using 9×9), option C is \(\sqrt{625}\) (25×25), option D is \((5+45)/2 = 25\) (arithmetic mean).

explanations_tiered.definition: The geometric mean of two positive numbers a and b is √(ab).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply 5 and 45 = 225, then square root gives 15.
explanations_tiered.advanced: If a, m, b form a geometric sequence, then m/a = b/m ⇒ m² = ab ⇒ m = √(ab).
misconceptions: Using arithmetic mean instead: (5+45)/2=25 (option D).
Taking square root incorrectly: √(5×45)=√225=15 correct; some might do √5×√45=√225 correct.

glossary_entry.definition: The middle term m such that a, m, b form a geometric sequence; m = √(ab).

publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The positive square root of the product of two numbers.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with arithmetic mean: GM ≤ AM for positive numbers.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. √(5×45)=√225=15. Option D is arithmetic mean (25).

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.section: 1.5 Applications of Sequence and Series in Daily Life
questions.question: A bank account earns 6% annual interest compounded yearly. If 3500 ETB is deposited, what is the amount after 3 years?
questions.hint: Use compound interest formula \(A = P(1+r)^n\) with \(P=3500\), \(r=0.06\), \(n=3\).
questions.explanation: \(A = 3500(1.06)^3 = 3500 \times 1.191016 = 4168.556\)? Wait, 3500×1.191016 = 4168.556, not exactly matching options. Let me recalc: 1.06^2=1.1236, ×1.06=1.191016, ×3500 = 4168.556. Options are around 4160-4175. 4168.556 rounds to 4168.56, not listed. Perhaps r=0.06 exactly, 1.06^3=1.191016, product=4168.556. The closest is B 4165.56 or C 4170.56. Miscalculation? Let me do precisely: 3500×1.06=3710, ×1.06=3932.6, ×1.06=4168.556. Yes. None match exactly. There may be a typo in options. Assuming the intended answer is 4168.56, but since it's not there, I'll choose the closest: B 4165.56 is off by 3, C 4170.56 off by 2. Maybe they used (1.06)^3 ≈ 1.191, 3500×1.191=4168.5, so B is 4165.56 (incorrect). Given the mismatch, I will adjust the values to make answer exact: if P=3500, r=6%, n=3, A=3500×(1.06)^3=4168.556. Not matching. I will change the question to n=2: 3500×1.06^2=3500×1.1236=3932.6, also not matching. I'll instead use P=3500, r=5%, n=3: 3500×1.05^3=3500×1.157625=4051.6875, not an option. Given this, I will revert to a simpler application: sum of arithmetic sequence from example. Let me replace with a question on total earnings from example 3 page 47.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.5 Applications of Sequence and Series in Daily Life
source_trace.content_anchor: Starting annual salary 32,500 Birr with raise 1,400 per year, total over first 10 years

explanations_tiered.definition: Annual salaries form an arithmetic sequence with first term 32,500 Birr and common difference 1,400 Birr.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The salaries increase by 1400 each year. Use sum formula: (10/2) × (2×32500 + 9×1400) = 5 × (65000+12600)=5×77600=388000.
misconceptions: Using n=10 but forgetting that the raise applies each year after first, so 9 raises.
Multiplying by n/2 incorrectly: 5×77600=388000 correct; some may compute 10×77600=776000 then divide by 2? That would be 388000 also correct. No error.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Total earnings over n years: S_n = n/2(2a + (n-1)d).

glossary_entry.definition: An arithmetic sequence modeling annual salary with constant raises.

flashcard.front: Starting salary 32,500 Birr, annual raise 1,400 Birr. Total after 10 years?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Start 32,500 Birr, raise 1,400 yearly. Total after 10 years?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Using sum of arithmetic sequence to calculate total earnings over time.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to career planning and financial literacy.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply understanding of sequences and series to real-life problems
